skill.name: frontend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Opinionated frontend development standards for modern React and TypeScript applications with Suspense-first data fetching, lazy loading, and feature-based architecture
skill.summary: Production-ready React and TypeScript guidelines with Suspense-first patterns, TanStack Query, and MUI v7 styling standards
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 649 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill consists entirely of Markdown documentation files containing code examples for educational purposes. Patterns like external_commands, scripts, filesystem, and network references are illustrative code snippets teaching React TypeScript development, not executable code. No actual security risks detected.

content.user_title: Build production React apps with modern patterns
content.value_statement: Frontend developers struggle with inconsistent patterns and technical debt in React codebases. This skill provides opinionated standards for Suspense-first data fetching, feature-based architecture, and TypeScript discipline.
actual_capabilities: Generate React components with Suspense-first data fetching using useSuspenseQuery
Enforce strict TypeScript patterns with explicit types and no any usage
Create feature-based project structures with proper code organization
Implement lazy loading patterns for routes and heavy components
Apply MUI v7 styling standards with inline sx props and theme integration
Design TanStack Router configurations with loader patterns
limitations: Focused on React TypeScript stack only, not other frontend frameworks
Assumes TanStack Query and MUI as primary dependencies
Opinionated approach may not fit all team preferences or legacy codebases
Requires React 18+ for Suspense and lazy loading patterns

use_cases.title: New React Component Development
use_cases.description: Generate production-ready React components following Suspense-first patterns with proper TypeScript types, lazy loading, and MUI styling
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers building new features in modern React applications

use_cases.title: Codebase Standardization
use_cases.description: Establish consistent architectural patterns across teams with feature-based organization, strict TypeScript, and unified styling approach
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and architects standardizing frontend development practices

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
use_cases.description: Refactor existing components to adopt useSuspenseQuery, remove isLoading conditionals, and implement proper lazy loading boundaries
use_cases.target_user: Senior developers modernizing inherited React codebases

prompt_templates.title: Create a Suspense Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React component that displays user profile data. Use useSuspenseQuery for fetching, wrap with SuspenseLoader, and style with MUI sx prop. Include TypeScript types for the user data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Learning modern React patterns with Suspense

prompt_templates.title: Feature-Based Structure Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a feature-based folder structure for a dashboard application with features: analytics, user management, and settings. Show the api, components, hooks, helpers, and types subdirectories for each feature with index.ts exports.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Organizing a new React project

prompt_templates.title: Lazy Loaded Route Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a TanStack Router configuration for a dashboard route with lazy loading. Include a loader function that returns breadcrumb metadata and uses cache-first query strategy for initial data fetch.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Setting up routing with data loading

prompt_templates.title: Refactor to Suspense Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this legacy component that uses useQuery with isLoading checks to use useSuspenseQuery instead. Remove all early returns and conditional loading states. Show the before and after code with explanations.

output_examples.input: Create a product listing component with Suspense
output: ProductList component created with useSuspenseQuery hook
Wrapped in SuspenseLoader boundary for automatic loading states
TypeScript interface defined for Product data type
MUI Grid layout with responsive sizing using v7 syntax
Cache-first query configuration for optimal performance

output_examples.input: How should I organize authentication feature code
output: Place auth API calls in api/authApi.ts with typed responses
Export public interfaces via index.ts barrel exports
Keep auth components in components/ subdirectory

best_practices: Always use useSuspenseQuery as the default data fetching pattern instead of useQuery with isLoading checks
Organize code by feature domain in features directory rather than by technical type
Lazy load any route component or heavy UI element like data grids and charts to optimize bundle size
anti_patterns: Early return patterns for loading states like if isLoading return null which breaks Suspense boundaries
Placing feature-specific business logic in the generic components directory instead of features
Making direct API calls inside components instead of using centralized feature API service layers

faq.question: Why use useSuspenseQuery instead of useQuery?
faq.answer: useSuspenseQuery eliminates isLoading conditionals by leveraging React Suspense boundaries. This results in cleaner component code with guaranteed data availability and consistent loading UX through centralized SuspenseLoader components.

faq.question: When should I use inline styles versus separate .styles.ts files?
faq.answer: Use inline styles with the sx prop for components under 100 lines. Create separate ComponentName.styles.ts files for larger components to maintain readability and separate concerns.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with existing React 17 codebases?
faq.answer: Suspense for data fetching requires React 18 or later. For React 17 projects, you can still apply the feature-based organization, TypeScript standards, and API layer patterns while using traditional useQuery patterns.

faq.question: What dependencies are required to follow these guidelines?
faq.answer: Core dependencies include React 18+, TypeScript, TanStack Query v5, TanStack Router, and MUI v7. Optional but recommended: React Hook Form and Zod for form handling.

faq.question: How do I handle error states with Suspense patterns?
faq.answer: Wrap Suspense boundaries with ErrorBoundary components from react-error-boundary or similar libraries. Use useMuiSnackbar hook for user-friendly error messages within components that trigger mutations.

faq.question: Is feature-based organization suitable for small projects?
faq.answer: For very small projects under 20 components, a simpler structure may suffice. However, feature-based organization scales predictably and establishes good habits early. Start with the pattern even for small projects.
